title: What biotechnology means
objective: Understand biotechnology as the use of organisms, cells or enzymes to make useful products and processes.
notes: Traditional biotechnology includes practices such as fermentation, curd formation, cheese making and antibiotic production.
Modern biotechnology uses DNA-level tools, especially recombinant DNA technology, PCR, vectors and engineered host cells.
The chapter connects two worlds: molecular manipulation of genes and large-scale production under controlled sterile conditions.
neetHook: If the question says organism/cell/enzyme plus useful product, it is asking the broad definition of biotechnology.
diagramPractice: Draw four boxes: organism/cell/enzyme -> controlled process -> product -> application.
check: Why is genetic engineering only one part of biotechnology, not the whole definition?

title: Principles of biotechnology
objective: Link genetic engineering with sterile bioprocessing.
notes: Genetic engineering changes the genetic material so that a desired trait or product can be obtained.
Sterile conditions are needed because unwanted microbes can outgrow the desired culture or contaminate the product.
A recombinant DNA experiment is complete only when the gene is expressed and the product is recovered safely.
neetHook: Two principles are repeatedly asked: genetic engineering and contamination-free maintenance.
diagramPractice: Draw desired gene -> vector -> host -> expression -> product, then add a sterile bioreactor around the host stage.
check: Which principle explains why bioreactors have pH, oxygen and foam control?

title: Tools of recombinant DNA technology
objective: Identify the role of each molecular tool.
notes: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences and can produce sticky or blunt ends.
DNA ligase seals compatible DNA ends by forming phosphodiester bonds.
Polymerase enzymes copy DNA; Taq polymerase is important for PCR because it is heat-stable.
Vectors carry DNA into host cells, and hosts maintain or express the recombinant DNA.
neetHook: Tool-function matching is a common NEET pattern: scissors, glue, copier, carrier, factory.
diagramPractice: Draw a toolbox with restriction enzyme, ligase, polymerase, vector and host cell.
check: Which tool copies DNA and which tool joins DNA?

title: Isolation of desired DNA
objective: Explain how DNA is released and purified from cells.
notes: The cell wall and membrane must be broken to release DNA.
Lysozyme acts on bacterial cell wall, cellulase on plant cell wall and chitinase on fungal cell wall.
Protease removes proteins, RNase removes RNA, and chilled ethanol precipitates DNA as thread-like material.
neetHook: Enzyme identity is a high-yield detail: lysozyme, cellulase, chitinase, protease and RNase.
diagramPractice: Draw a test tube with cell lysate, ethanol layer and visible DNA threads.
check: Why is chilled ethanol added during DNA isolation?

title: Restriction enzymes and palindromes
objective: Understand recognition sequences, EcoRI, sticky ends and blunt ends.
notes: Restriction endonucleases cut DNA internally at specific recognition sequences.
Many recognition sequences are palindromic when read in the 5' to 3' direction on both strands.
EcoRI recognises GAATTC and produces sticky ends that can pair with complementary sticky ends.
neetHook: EcoRI and palindromic sequence interpretation are classic line-based questions.
diagramPractice: Write 5'-GAATTC-3' above 3'-CTTAAG-5' and mark the EcoRI cut.
check: What makes sticky ends useful for recombinant DNA formation?

title: PCR amplification
objective: Explain denaturation, annealing, extension and exponential copy number.
notes: Denaturation separates DNA strands at high temperature.
Annealing allows primers to bind to complementary target sequences.
Extension uses Taq polymerase at about 72 C to synthesize new DNA strands.
Ideal amplification follows 2^n, where n is the number of cycles.
neetHook: Cycle-temperature-function questions are very common.
diagramPractice: Draw one PCR cycle as three panels with temperatures: 95 C, 50-65 C, 72 C.
check: How many copies form from one DNA molecule after 8 ideal cycles?

title: Ligation and recombinant DNA formation
objective: Show how a gene insert joins with a vector.
notes: The vector and insert should have compatible ends, often created by the same restriction enzyme.
DNA ligase seals nicks in the sugar-phosphate backbone.
A plasmid carrying a foreign DNA insert is called a recombinant plasmid.
neetHook: Compatible sticky ends plus ligase equals recombinant DNA.
diagramPractice: Draw an open plasmid, gene insert and final closed recombinant plasmid.
check: Why is the same restriction enzyme often used for the vector and insert?

title: Cloning vectors
objective: Explain ori, selectable marker, cloning site and copy number.
notes: ori allows the vector to replicate in the host cell.
Selectable markers help identify transformed cells.
A cloning site contains restriction sites where foreign DNA can be inserted.
Copy number affects how many copies of the vector exist in a cell.
neetHook: Vector feature-function matching appears often.
diagramPractice: Draw a circular plasmid map with ori, marker, restriction site and gene insert.
check: What is the role of ori in a cloning vector?

title: Competent host and transformation
objective: Understand how recombinant DNA enters host cells.
notes: Competent cells are able to take up foreign DNA.
Bacterial cells can be made competent using CaCl2 treatment followed by heat shock.
Microinjection, gene gun and disarmed pathogen vectors are other transfer approaches.
Agrobacterium-mediated transfer is important in plant biotechnology.
neetHook: Transformation methods are often asked as direct one-line facts.
diagramPractice: Draw plasmid entry into a bacterial cell after a heat shock pulse.
check: Why must host cells be made competent?

title: Selection of recombinants
objective: Differentiate transformed, non-transformed, recombinant and non-recombinant cells.
notes: Non-transformed cells do not receive the vector and usually fail to survive selection.
Transformed cells receive the vector, but may or may not contain the desired insert.
Insertional inactivation occurs when inserted DNA disrupts a marker gene.
Blue-white screening uses this marker disruption idea to identify recombinant colonies.
neetHook: Transformed is not always recombinant. That distinction is a common trap.
diagramPractice: Draw a decision tree: no vector, vector without insert, vector with insert.
check: Why can a transformed cell still be non-recombinant?

title: Gel electrophoresis and elution
objective: Explain DNA fragment separation by size.
notes: DNA is negatively charged and moves toward the positive electrode.
Agarose gel acts like a molecular sieve.
Smaller fragments move farther than larger fragments.
Elution means recovering the desired DNA fragment from the gel.
neetHook: The direction of DNA movement and size-distance relation are extremely high-yield.
diagramPractice: Draw wells near the negative electrode and bands moving toward the positive side.
check: Which travels farther: 500 bp or 5000 bp DNA?

title: Bioreactors
objective: Describe large-scale production in a stirred-tank bioreactor.
notes: Bioreactors provide optimal conditions for microbial or cell growth.
A stirred-tank bioreactor has an agitator/impeller for mixing and a sparger for air supply.
Temperature, pH, oxygen and foam are monitored and controlled.
Small lab cultures are scaled up to pilot and industrial levels.
neetHook: Sparger and impeller functions are frequently tested.
diagramPractice: Draw a labelled stirred tank with impeller, sparger, pH, temperature and sampling port.
check: What is the role of a sparger?

title: Downstream processing
objective: Trace what happens after product formation.
notes: After expression, the product is separated from cells and culture medium.
The product is purified, formulated and quality tested.
For therapeutic products, clinical testing may be required before use.
Packaging and release happen only after quality control.
neetHook: Downstream processing is often hidden in options as separation, purification and formulation.
diagramPractice: Draw culture broth -> separation -> purification -> formulation -> QC -> vial.
check: Why is gene expression not the final step in biotechnology production?

title: Genetically engineered insulin
objective: Explain recombinant human insulin production.
notes: Earlier insulin from animals could cause immune reactions in some patients.
Proinsulin contains A-chain, B-chain and C-peptide.
Mature insulin lacks C-peptide and has A and B chains linked by disulphide bonds.
Recombinant microbes can produce insulin chains that are purified and combined.
neetHook: C-peptide is present in proinsulin, absent in mature insulin.
diagramPractice: Draw proinsulin -> C-peptide removal -> A/B chains linked -> insulin vial.
check: Which peptide is removed during insulin maturation?

title: Bt cotton
objective: Explain Cry protein activation and pest resistance.
notes: Bt genes come from Bacillus thuringiensis.
Cry proteins are produced as inactive protoxins.
The alkaline gut of the insect activates the protoxin.
The active toxin damages midgut epithelial cells, killing the pest.
neetHook: Activation happens in alkaline insect gut, not inside the plant.
diagramPractice: Draw plant leaf -> larva feeding -> alkaline gut -> active toxin -> midgut damage.
check: Why is Bt protoxin safe inside the plant but harmful to target insects?

title: Gene therapy
objective: Understand ADA deficiency and somatic gene therapy at student level.
notes: Gene therapy introduces a functional gene to compensate for a defective one.
ADA deficiency is the classic example.
Ex vivo therapy removes cells, corrects them outside the body and returns them.
Somatic gene therapy affects body cells and is not inherited.
neetHook: ADA deficiency plus ex vivo correction is the key NCERT memory pair.
diagramPractice: Draw patient cell removed -> corrected with vector -> returned to patient.
check: Why is somatic gene therapy not passed to offspring?

title: RNA interference
objective: Explain dsRNA-mediated gene silencing.
notes: RNAi is a cellular method of silencing genes.
Double-stranded RNA leads to degradation or blocking of complementary mRNA.
The DNA gene remains, but its message is not translated.
NCERT uses nematode-resistant tobacco as an example.
neetHook: RNAi works at the RNA level; it does not cut the DNA gene.
diagramPractice: Draw dsRNA -> small RNA -> target mRNA cut -> protein absent.
check: What molecule is degraded during RNA interference?

title: Molecular diagnosis
objective: Compare PCR, ELISA and DNA probes.
notes: PCR detects small amounts of pathogen nucleic acid by amplification.
ELISA detects antigen-antibody interaction using a measurable signal.
DNA probes detect complementary target sequences.
Early diagnosis improves treatment decisions because markers may be low initially.
neetHook: ELISA is antigen-antibody; DNA probe is complementary base pairing.
diagramPractice: Draw PCR tube, ELISA plate and DNA probe binding target DNA.
check: Which technique is based on antigen-antibody interaction?

title: Transgenic animals
objective: Know why transgenic animals are made.
notes: Transgenic animals carry foreign genes.
They can help study gene regulation and disease models.
They can be used for biological product production and vaccine/safety testing.
Ethical and welfare concerns must be considered.
neetHook: Transgenic animals are not only for producing products; they also model disease and test safety.
diagramPractice: Draw animal cell with inserted gene -> trait/model/product output.
check: Give two uses of transgenic animals.

title: Ethical issues, patents, biopiracy and GEAC
objective: Understand responsible biotechnology.
notes: GM organisms must be evaluated for biosafety and environmental impact.
Patents can protect inventions but may raise access and ownership questions.
Biopiracy means unfair use of bioresources or traditional knowledge.
GEAC is associated with appraisal of genetically engineered organisms and products in India.
neetHook: GEAC, biosafety and biopiracy are short but frequently asked topics.
diagramPractice: Draw research -> biosafety data -> GEAC appraisal -> monitored release.
check: What is biopiracy?
